tossups.question: Denis Lavant (“duh-NEE lah-VAWN”) mimics one of these people while following him around Marseille in the 2014 sequel to the short film Walker, which had also starred Lee Kang-sheng as one of these people. A huge apparition of one of these people haunts the protagonist of the surreal horror film The Boxer’s Omen. An army musician who is the lone survivor of an attack at the end of World War II impersonates one of these people in the 1956 film The Burmese Harp. In a depressing close-up from a 2003 film, the tears of one of these people stain paper cutouts that are attached to his ears, mouth, and eyes. A boy who is punished for torturing a (*) fish, frog, and snake lives with one of these people in a floating building in the film Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter… and Spring. A group of these people who wander through a forest provide backup for a female fugitive in King Hu’s best-known wuxia film. For 10 points, what sort of people reside in the title location of The 36th Chamber of Shaolin?
tossups.question_sanitized: Denis Lavant ("duh-NEE lah-VAWN") mimics one of these people while following him around Marseille in the 2014 sequel to the short film Walker, which had also starred Lee Kang-sheng as one of these people. A huge apparition of one of these people haunts the protagonist of the surreal horror film The Boxer's Omen. An army musician who is the lone survivor of an attack at the end of World War II impersonates one of these people in the 1956 film The Burmese Harp. In a depressing close-up from a 2003 film, the tears of one of these people stain paper cutouts that are attached to his ears, mouth, and eyes. A boy who is punished for torturing a (*) fish, frog, and snake lives with one of these people in a floating building in the film Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter... and Spring. A group of these people who wander through a forest provide backup for a female fugitive in King Hu's best-known wuxia film. For 10 points, what sort of people reside in the title location of The 36th Chamber of Shaolin?
tossups.answer: Buddhist monks [prompt on monks by asking “of what religion?”; prompt on martial artists; reject “priests”] (The film in the first line is Journey to the West by Tsai Ming-liang. The King Hu film is A Touch of Zen.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Buddhist monks [prompt on monks by asking "of what religion?"; prompt on martial artists; reject "priests"] (The film in the first line is Journey to the West by Tsai Ming-liang. The King Hu film is A Touch of Zen.)

tossups.question: This is the second word in the title of an anthology containing a story about a moneylender who goes mad after finding a stolen locket of Mohammad’s hair. This is the second word in the title of a novel that sees the protagonists travel from Mykonos to London via magic doors. Mohsin Hamid wrote about the (*) refugees Nadia and Saeed in a novel titled Exit[this word]. This is the last word in the title of a novel centering on a cloud-somersaulting figure with seventy-two transformations. This word is paired with “East” in the title of a Salman Rushdie collection. For 10 points, name this cardinal direction that titles the classic Chinese novel about Sun Wukong’s trip to India.
tossups.question_sanitized: This is the second word in the title of an anthology containing a story about a moneylender who goes mad after finding a stolen locket of Mohammad's hair. This is the second word in the title of a novel that sees the protagonists travel from Mykonos to London via magic doors. Mohsin Hamid wrote about the (*) refugees Nadia and Saeed in a novel titled Exit[this word]. This is the last word in the title of a novel centering on a cloud-somersaulting figure with seventy-two transformations. This word is paired with "East" in the title of a Salman Rushdie collection. For 10 points, name this cardinal direction that titles the classic Chinese novel about Sun Wukong's trip to India.
tossups.answer_sanitized: west [accept Exit West or Journey to the West]

tossups.question: During a fight, a character in this novel is captured after he is hit in the head by a bracelet thrown from heaven. In this novel, the daughter of a village elder is kidnapped by her hard-working suitor who consumes more food than the farm produces. In a later supplement to this novel, one character is able to time travel after getting trapped in a dream world by a fish demon. A character in this novel loses a bet after he is unable to (*) somersault out of a hand. The task of cleaning altars is given to a character in this novel who uses a nine-toothed rake as a weapon. A character in this novel born from a stone egg is freed from the mountain he is trapped under after agreeing to help a monk retrieve Buddhist texts. For 10 points, name this novel in which Tang Sanzang and Sun Wukong, the Monkey King, travel to India.
tossups.question_sanitized: During a fight, a character in this novel is captured after he is hit in the head by a bracelet thrown from heaven. In this novel, the daughter of a village elder is kidnapped by her hard-working suitor who consumes more food than the farm produces. In a later supplement to this novel, one character is able to time travel after getting trapped in a dream world by a fish demon. A character in this novel loses a bet after he is unable to (*) somersault out of a hand. The task of cleaning altars is given to a character in this novel who uses a nine-toothed rake as a weapon. A character in this novel born from a stone egg is freed from the mountain he is trapped under after agreeing to help a monk retrieve Buddhist texts. For 10 points, name this novel in which Tang Sanzang and Sun Wukong, the Monkey King, travel to India.
tossups.answer: Journey to the West [or Xī Yóu Jì; accept Monkey: A Folk-Tale of China before “Monkey” is said]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Journey to the West [or Xi You Ji; accept Monkey: A Folk-Tale of China before "Monkey" is said]

bonuses.leadin: An author says "no, no, [this figure] shall not be shot" after he recalls a "young Gentleman of good family" who runs around town shooting animals. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: An author says "no, no, [this figure] shall not be shot" after he recalls a "young Gentleman of good family" who runs around town shooting animals. For 10 points each:
parts: Name or describe this figure who an author "used to go out and buy oysters [for], lest the servants having that trouble should take a dislike to the poor creature."

Boswe<u>ll employed</u> an anecdotal style in a travel journal recounting a journey to this place he took with Johnson, as opposed to Johnson's own serious account of the trip.

bonuses.leadin: In one work, this character helps his lover poison her husband who is nicknamed "Three Inches of Mulberry Bark". For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In one work, this character helps his lover poison her husband who is nicknamed "Three Inches of Mulberry Bark". For 10 points each:
parts: Name this male character who is brutally killed by Wu Song in revenge for Wu Dalang’s (“tah-lahngz”) death in Water Margin. In another novel, this character dies of aphrodisiac overdose after his sex-starved wife gives him three pills instead of just one.

Although Wu Song kills both his sister-in-law Pan Jinlian (“pan jin-l’yen”) and her lover Ximen Qing (“shee-men ching”) in the original plot of Water Margin, he fails to kill Qing in this infamously lewd spin-off. This novel takes its Chinese name from the given names of its three main female characters.

This other Ming-dynasty Classical Novel originates in popular tales about the Buddhist monk Xuanzang’s (“shwen-tsahng”) pilgrimage to India to obtain sacred texts. Arthur Waley’s abridged translation of this novel was titled Monkey.
parts_sanitized: Name this male character who is brutally killed by Wu Song in revenge for Wu Dalang's ("tah-lahngz") death in Water Margin. In another novel, this character dies of aphrodisiac overdose after his sex-starved wife gives him three pills instead of just one.

Although Wu Song kills both his sister-in-law Pan Jinlian ("pan jin-l'yen") and her lover Ximen Qing ("shee-men ching") in the original plot of Water Margin, he fails to kill Qing in this infamously lewd spin-off. This novel takes its Chinese name from the given names of its three main female characters.

This other Ming-dynasty Classical Novel originates in popular tales about the Buddhist monk Xuanzang's ("shwen-tsahng") pilgrimage to India to obtain sacred texts. Arthur Waley's abridged translation of this novel was titled Monkey.
answers: Plum in the Golden Vase [or The Golden Lotus; or Jin Ping Mei]
answers_sanitized: Ximen Qing ("shee-men ching") [accept either underlined part]
Plum in the Golden Vase [or The Golden Lotus; or Jin Ping Mei]
Journey to the West [or Xi You Ji]

bonuses.leadin: In response to the desertion of his wife Emelda, this character becomes a belligerent drunk, which ironically earns him the respect of his peers. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this carpenter who gives up on making the “thing without a name” after he is imprisoned for theft. This carpenter appears in a collection that also features the prophet Man-Man and the poet B. Wordsworth.
Although there is no Mr. Popo, this classic novel by Wu Cheng’en about Xuanzang’s pilgrimage to India does have characters named the Ox King and Son Goku.

The protagonist of this Natsume Soseki novel takes a job as a math teacher on Shikoku, where he works alongside “Radish” and “Squash.” This novel depicts the title character’s struggle with the teacher “Red Shirt.”
answers: Journey to the West [or Xi You Ji; accept Monkey]
Botchan (The theme of this bonus is “literary characters who share their name with DBZ characters.” Grumble if you will—Hippocleides doesn’t care.)
answers_sanitized: Mr. Popo (from Miguel Street by V.S. Naipaul)

bonuses.leadin: A short story in Xuan (“shwen”) Juliana Wang’s collection Home Remedies begins by quoting an aphorism that “when you’re young you shouldn’t read” this work. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this major work of classical Chinese literature in which the Monkey King and Tripiṭaka travel to Vulture Peak to receive Buddhist scriptures. Arthur Waley translated this text under the title Monkey.

In Wang’s short story, the grandmaster Tutu claims to have killed one of these animals as a boy. In Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Dǒng Xǐ (“dong shee”) kills a character whose name references these animals and sends that character’s head to Sūn Cè (“swun tsuh”).

Wǔ Sōng (“oo song”), a member of a group of this many characters, kills a tiger with his fists in Water Margin. He is a member of the Stars of Destiny, who meet at Mount Liáng (“lʼyahng”) and contain this many characters.
answers: Journey to the West [or Xī Yóu Jì or Hsi-yu Chi]
white tiger [or báihǔ; accept Yán Báihǔ; prompt on tiger, lǎohǔ, big cats, or felines]
108 [or yībǎilíngbā; accept answers that include a noun, such as 108 Stars of Destiny or 108 Heroes; accept 36 Heavenly Spirits]

parts: Name this action. A character born out of a stone egg becomes a king after performing this action to enter a mountain cave.

This character becomes the king of his group after jumping through a waterfall in the first chapter of a long novel. A translation by Arthur Waley is titled for this character, who wields a staff that allows him to shrink and grow to any size.
Sūn Wùkōng assists Tang Sanzang on his quest to bring back Buddhist scriptures from India in this classic Chinese novel by Wú Chéng’ēn.

bonuses.leadin: Zhū Yù’s performance piece entitled Eating People, in which he claimed to have eaten a human fetus, references a story by this author. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who created a protagonist who sees the words “eat people” written between the lines of Confucian classics in his short story “Diary of a Madman.”
Demons attempt to eat the monk’s Táng Sānzàng’s flesh multiple times in this member of the Four Classic Chinese novels, in which Sūn Wùkōng and Pigsy join the title trip.
In a novel, this author wrote of the “special investigator” Dīng Gōu’er (“ding goh-arr”), who goes to investigate cannibalism and, like Zhū Yù, eats a dish that may be a real infant or a facsimile.

Journey to the West [or Xī Yóu Jì; accept Monkey: A Folk-Tale of China]
Mò Yán [or Guǎn Móyè] (The novel is The Republic of Wine.)

bonuses.leadin: In the preface to a translated edition to this book, an author stated that his edition only included 30 of its 100 chapters to focus more on the specifics of the included chapters. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this book translated by Arthur Waley which he dubbed Monkey after the character Sun Wukong.

This translator of the Upanishads provided a German translation of numerous Indian folk tales in the collection Hitopadesa. He may be better known for analyzing the natural, physical, anthropological, and psychological types of religion in his Gifford Lectures.

Donald Keene gave the subtitle Its Background and Importance to his translation of this author's puppet play The Battles of Coxinga. Keene also published an edition of Four Major Plays of [this author], including The Uprooted Pine and The Love Suicides of Sonezaki.
answers_sanitized: Journey to the West
Max Muller
Chikamatsu Monzaemon [accept Sugimori Nobumori]

parts: Name this Chinese novel about a motley trio who accompany the monk Tang Sanzang on the title expedition.

In Journey to the West, Sun Wukong is given his magical shape-changing staff by the king of the eastern sea, who is one of these creatures. Tang Sanzang rides a white horse who is also one of these creatures.

The aforementioned spin-off is based on an episode where Sun Wukong turns into a fly and enters a demon’s body to beat her up, so that she will give up one of these items, which allows the group to pass flaming mountains.

dragons [or long]
(a banana leaf) fan [or sanjie bajiao shan]

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about the Chinese literary character Sun Wukong, for 10 points each.
parts: Sun Wukong is the simian hero of this Chinese literary classic by Wu Cheng'in, in which he travels away from China with Xuanzang and several others to obtain Buddhist scriptures from India.
Sun Wukong hatched from an egg made of this unusual substance. A large one forsaken by a goddess appears in, and provides an alternate title, for Dream of the Red Chamber.
This Buddhist sage and tutor conferred the name "Sun Wukong" upon the Monkey King to reward him for his awareness of the vacuity of the universe.
answers: Journey to the West [or Monkey; or Xi you ji]

stone [or rock; or boulder]
Patriarch Subodhi [or Subhuti]

bonuses.leadin: Identify some Chinese novels, for 10 points each.
parts: This novel follows a Buddhist monk, Tripitaka who tries to bring Buddhism to China. He is joined by Sandy, Pigsy and the Monkey King.

This one-hundred chapter novel by Shi Nai’an is based on the exploits of the outlaw Song Jiang and his companions during the Song dynasty. It sees the one-hundred-and-eight stars of destiny fight the forces of Gao Qiu.

Tris Cole wants you to know that this novel uses several themes from the Water Margin although it is better known for its depiction of sexual fetishes. The sex could get a bit dangerous as the concubine Li Ping’er bleeds to death and the protagonist Ximen Qing dies after an overdose of aphrodisiacs.
answers: Journey to the West [or Xī Yóu Jì; or Hsi-yu chi; prompt on “Monkey”, which is an abridged translation]
Water Margin [or Shuǐ Hǔ Zhuàn; or Outlaws of the Marsh and equivalent translations; or All Men Are Brothers; or The Marshes of Mount Liang]
The Plum in the Golden Vase [accept equivalent translations; or Jin Ping Mei; or The Golden Lotus]

The Monkey King acquired his magic iron staff from the "sea treasury" of this deity, who rules over the Pacific Ocean from his palace guarded by shrimps and crabs.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about Buddhism in China, for 10 points each.
parts: This painting shows the founders of Daoism, Confucianism, and Buddhism performing the title action, and respectively reacting to sweet, sour, and bitter sensations.
A milestone in Chinese Buddhism was this event, which Xuanzang undertook in 646. It resulted in descriptions of Tashkent, Samarkand, and Bactria as well as the retrieval of several Sanskrit sutras.
This form of Buddhism believes in the practice of nembutsu, which involves invoking the name of Amitabha. It was founded in China by Huiyuan.
answers: the journey to the west [or the pilgrimage to India or equivalents to either]
answers_sanitized: The Three Vinegar-Tasters
the journey to the west [or the pilgrimage to India or equivalents to either]
Pure Land Buddhism [or Qingtu]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about Buddhism in China, for 10 points each.
parts_sanitized: This painting shows the founders of Daoism, Confucianism, and Buddhism performing the title action, and respectively reacting to sweet, sour, and bitter sensations.
A milestone in Chinese Buddhism was this event, which Xuanzang undertook in 646. It resulted in descriptions of Tashkent, Samarkand, and Bactria as well as the retrieval of several Sanskrit sutras.
This form of Buddhism believes in the practice of nembutsu, which involves invoking the name of Amitabha. It was founded in China by Huiyuan.

bonuses.leadin: Instead of playing an ill-conceived literature tossup on the Ming Dynasty, answer this bonus about works from that time period, for 10 points each:
parts: Vulture Peak in India is the destination for the characters in this work, one of the Four Great Novels. It sees demoted demigods Pigsy and Sandy help on a quest to retrieve some Buddhist scriptures from India led by Sun Wukong, whose animal form provides this work's alternate title.
The fall of the Song Dynasty is a subplot in this most famous of Tang Xianzu’s “Four Dreams,” in which Du Liniang dies after a petal wakes her from her dream romance with the scholar Liu Mengmei.
The Peony Pavilion was later adapted into Kunqu, an early version of this type of work. By far the most important variety of this work utilizes the jinghu and has roles like Jin, Dan, and Sheng.
answers_sanitized: Journey to the West [or Monkey; or Xiyouji]
The Peony Pavilion
Chinese opera
